coredata.dc:title: Detection of clenbuterol in meat samples using a molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensor with MnFe2O4-CQDs composite material
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Chong
Li, Yuran
coredata.dc:description: Clenbuterol hydrochloride (CLB) is a β-adrenergic agonizts that can stimulate specific receptors in muscles, leading to an increase in protein synthesis and lipid solubility, and consequently muscle fiber growth. As a result, it is a prohibited substance in sports. In this study, an electrochemical sensor based on molecular imprinting technology was proposed for high-sensitivity detection of CLB. MnFe2O4-carbon quantum dots (CQDs) composite material was prepared by a one-step solvent-thermal method and used as the electrode modification material to prepare the modified electrode. MIP (molecularly imprinted polymer) film was electro-polymerized using 3-aminophenylboronic acid as the functional monomer and CLB as the template molecule with the CV (cyclic voltammetry) technique. After washing out the CLB template molecule with an acetic acid-sodium acetate buffer solution, an MIP/MnFe2O4-CQDs-CS/GCE sensor with specific recognition performance for CLB was obtained. The factors that affect the performance of the sensor were investigated, including sensitivity, selectivity, stability, reproducibility, and repeatability. The research results showed that under the optimal conditions, the constructed sensor could detect CLB in the concentration range of 10 nM to 10 mM, with a calculated LOD of 0.041 μM.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Clenbuterol hydrochloride
β-adrenergic agonizts
Electrochemical sensor
Molecular imprinting
Voltammetry

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectra of MnFe2O4 and MnFe2O4-CQDs.
XRD patterns of MnFe2O4 and MnFe2O4-CQDs.
EIS data of bare GCE, MnFe2O4-CQDs-CS/GCE, MnFe2O4-CS/GCE, CQDs-CS/GCE, MIP/MnFe2O4-CQDs-CS/GCE and CLB/MIP/MnFe2O4-CQDs-CS/GCE after rebinding of 50 μM CLB.
(A) Effect of molar ratio of template molecule to monomer (1:2, 1:1, 2:1); (B) Effect of elution time; (C) Effect of adsorption time.
(A) DPV curves of MIP/MnFe2O4-CQDs-CS/GCE towards 10 nM to 10 mM of CLB. (B) Plots of concentrations against the peak current.
Comparison of MIP/MnFe2O4-CQDs-CS/GCE sensor with other reported sensors for CLB detection.

head.ce:title: Detection of clenbuterol in meat samples using a molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensor with MnFe2O4-CQDs composite material

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Physical Education, Hunan International Economics University, Changsha 410205, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Physical Education, Hunan International Economics University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Physical Education, Hunan International Economics University, Changsha, 410205, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Physical Education Teaching and Research Office, Hunan SANY Polytechnic College, Changsha 410129, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Physical Education Teaching and Research Office, Hunan SANY Polytechnic College

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Physical Education Teaching and Research Office, Hunan SANY Polytechnic College, Changsha 410129, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Clenbuterol hydrochloride (CLB) is a β-adrenergic agonizts that can stimulate specific receptors in muscles, leading to an increase in protein synthesis and lipid solubility, and consequently muscle fiber growth. As a result, it is a prohibited substance in sports. In this study, an electrochemical sensor based on molecular imprinting technology was proposed for high-sensitivity detection of CLB. MnFe2O4-carbon quantum dots (CQDs) composite material was prepared by a one-step solvent-thermal method and used as the electrode modification material to prepare the modified electrode. MIP (molecularly imprinted polymer) film was electro-polymerized using 3-aminophenylboronic acid as the functional monomer and CLB as the template molecule with the CV (cyclic voltammetry) technique. After washing out the CLB template molecule with an acetic acid-sodium acetate buffer solution, an MIP/MnFe2O4-CQDs-CS/GCE sensor with specific recognition performance for CLB was obtained. The factors that affect the performance of the sensor were investigated, including sensitivity, selectivity, stability, reproducibility, and repeatability. The research results showed that under the optimal conditions, the constructed sensor could detect CLB in the concentration range of 10 nM to 10 mM, with a calculated LOD of 0.041 μM.

ce:section.ce:para: β-adrenergic agonizts, a class of drugs that stimulate β-receptors, were originally developed as therapeutic medications in the 1960s. These drugs produce bronchodilation by exciting β-receptors on airway smooth muscle, which helps to alleviate symptoms of airway smooth muscle spasm and breathing difficulties in acute asthma attacks [1–3]. As such, they are the preferred medications for patients with acute asthma. The main reason for the use of β-adrenergic agonizts in industries such as veterinary medicine and feed additives is due to consumer preferences for low-fat meat and a dislike for high-fat foods, driven by a desire for better health. This consumer mindset has driven livestock enterprises to urgently increase the lean meat content of animal products to ensure higher economic efficiency and avoid economic losses due to high-fat meat products [4–7]. Thus, β-adrenergic agonizts have rapidly developed in the livestock industry. Feeding animals with β-adrenergic agonizts not only increases feed conversion efficiency, but also significantly increases lean meat content and muscle protein content. Moreover, the bright red color of the meat is particularly attractive to consumers. β-adrenergic agonizts greatly improve the performance of livestock and categorize the tender meat of processed animals as intermediate. Johnson et al. [8] reviewed the impact of β-adrenergic agonizts on beef cattle production and found that when used in combination with concentrated feed, β-adrenergic agonizts can continuously improve the growth performance of beef cattle and increase muscle growth. In addition to being widely used in the livestock industry, the fat-reducing effect of β-adrenergic agonizts is highly sought after by fitness enthusiasts and bodybuilders [9–13]. After consuming β-adrenergic agonizts, the body temperature increases, heart rate accelerates, and calorie consumption increases, while glycogen breakdown is enhanced to promote fat metabolism and ultimately achieve the goal of reducing body fat [14].

β-adrenergic agonizts can be divided into three main classes based on their structure: phenylethylamines, phenols, and catechols, totaling 16 different types. The most commonly used β-adrenergic agonizts in the market are Clenbuterol hydrochloride (CLB), Ractopamine (RAC), and Terbutaline sulfate (TRA). Due to the serious threat that β-adrenergic agonizts pose to human health, several countries including China and the European Union have banned their use [15–17]. Developed countries, such as Europe and America, not only prohibit the use of β-adrenergic agonizts in livestock production but have also established maximum residue limits for these substances in animal products, in cooperation with the World Health Organization since 1986. The Medical Committee of the International Olympic Committee has also explicitly prohibited the use of CLB in the daily training and treatment of diseases in athletes participating in the 25th Barcelona Olympic Games. Despite various modern analytical techniques, such as molecular imprinting analysis, SPR analysis, colorimetric analysis, and liquid chromatography/tandem mass spectrometry analysis, having been reported for the determination of β-adrenergic agonist molecules, they require advanced technical expertize and are costly and time-consuming [18–21]. In the current era of information, not only are the requirements for automation, complexity, and environmental adaptability of various control systems increasing, but the demand for information acquisition is also increasing [22]. This has made sensing technology a rising star in the research field. To meet the demand for sensitive and rapid detection, higher requirements have been proposed for the measurement accuracy, response speed, and reliability of sensors, as well as for integration, miniaturization, intelligence, networking, and long-distance signal transmission. Sensing technology involves many interdisciplinary, cross-industry, and cross-domain theoretical foundations. Sensors are an important new method in modern analytical technology, and they are rapidly developing and widely used in the detection of biological organisms, tissues, cells, biomolecules, and various small molecules [23].

In recent years, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have gained attention as promising materials for various applications, including electrochemical sensing, due to their unique properties such as high conductivity, excellent biocompatibility, and strong fluorescence [24]. Moreover, CQDs possess large specific surface area and abundant functional groups, which can enhance the performance of sensors by promoting electron transfer and providing more active sites for target molecules [25].

Manganese ferrite (MnFe2O4) nanoparticles have been widely investigated for their applications in various fields, such as catalysis, magnetic resonance imaging, and electrochemical sensing, due to their excellent magnetic properties, biocompatibility, and high surface area [26]. These characteristics make MnFe2O4 a promising candidate for the construction of electrochemical sensors. In addition, CQDs have attracted significant attention as an emerging nanomaterial for electrochemical sensing applications owing to their excellent electrical conductivity, biocompatibility, and tunable optical properties [27]. The combination of MnFe2O4 and CQDs can provide synergistic effects, resulting in enhanced electrochemical performance and sensitivity. Recent studies have demonstrated the successful application of MnFe2O4-CQD composites in electrochemical sensors. For instance, a MnFe2O4-CQD modified electrode was reported to exhibit high sensitivity and selectivity for the electrochemical detection of dopamine [28]. Another study showed that a MnFe2O4-CQD composite material significantly improved the electrocatalytic activity of a glucose sensor, resulting in enhanced sensitivity and a wide linear range. These findings support the potential of MnFe2O4-CQDs as an effective electrode modification material for molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensors.

Electrochemical sensing is a detection platform that combines chemical reaction signals with sensing technology. An electrochemical sensor is a type of sensor that is based on the electrochemical properties of the analyte and converts the chemical quantity of the analyte into an electrical quantity for sensing detection [29,30]. The earliest electrochemical sensors were developed in the 1950s for oxygen monitoring. These sensors work by reacting with the target gas and producing an electric signal that is proportional to the gas concentration. Electrochemical sensors have high selectivity, sensitivity, and fast detection speed for target detection, and the concentration of the detection object is linearly related to the strength of the electrical signal produced. Electrochemical sensors are widely used for CLB detection. Lian et al. [31] used nitrogen-doped carbon nanotube (C3N4NTs)/ionic liquid (ILs) nanocomposites to prepare electrochemical sensing electrodes for sensitive analysis of CLB. Silva et al. [32] assembled acid-treated single-walled carbon nanotubes on graphene oxide and dispersed them in a Nafion solution by ultrasound to form composite nanomaterials, which were then used to modify a glassy carbon electrode. The resulting electrochemical sensor exhibited good linear relationships between the peak current and the CLB concentration in the range of 1.0 × 10−8–6.0 × 10−6 M, with a detection limit of 6.0 × 10−9 M. Compared with conventional analytical methods such as ELISA kits and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), the electrochemical sensor was successfully used to determine the CLB content in pork and pork liver with a recovery rate of 96.4–104.2 %. Wang et al. [33] reported a highly sensitive CLB electrochemical sensor that was developed by modifying a glassy carbon electrode with acetylene black (AB) nanoparticles. The linear range of this sensor was 1 μg/L to 2 mg/L, and the detection limit was 0.5 μg/L. This method was successfully applied to detect ractopamine in pork samples, with a recovery rate between 93.0 % and 106.3 %.

Molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensors (MIPES) have been developed to achieve high sensitivity and specificity in the recognition of analytes. The technique combines the specificity of molecular imprinting with the high sensitivity of electrochemical sensors, making it applicable in fields such as food, pharmaceuticals, and environmental monitoring [34]. For instance, Que et al. [35] developed an enzyme-enhanced MIPES for the detection of streptomycin in food samples. The MIPES was prepared by co-polymerizing aniline and o-phenylenediamine on a gold substrate using streptomycin as the template, and detected using differential pulse voltammetry (DPV). The catalytic current signal was found to be proportional to the concentration of streptomycin in the range of 0.01–10 ng/mL with a limit of detection (LOD) of 7.0 pg/mL. The MIPES was used to detect streptomycin in honey and milk samples with recovery rates ranging from 82 % to 124.2 %. Similarly, Menon et al. [36] developed a MIPES for the detection of acetaminophen using gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) deposited on a gold electrode surface to enhance the electrochemical signal. The MIPES was prepared by electropolymerizing o-aminophenol as the functional monomer and acetaminophen as the template molecule on the AuNPs/GE surface. The optimized MIPES exhibited a linear relationship between the peak current and acetaminophen concentration in the range of 0.5–45 μM with an LOD of 2.3 nM. The MIPES demonstrated excellent selectivity and reproducibility and was successfully used to analyze acetaminophen in pharmaceutical formulations and biological fluids. Ann et al. [37] developed a highly selective MIPES for the detection of 2,4-dichlorophenol (2.4-DCP) using 3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene as the functional monomer. The MIPES was prepared by electropolymerizing the monomer on a carbon fiber paper electrode surface in the presence of 2.4-DCP. The optimized MIPES exhibited a linear response in the range of 0.21–300 nM with an LOD of 0.07 nM and was effectively used to detect 2.4-DCP in real water samples. In this study, MnFe2O4-CQDs composite materials were prepared by a one-step solvothermal method using iron chloride, manganese chloride, and pomelo peel powder as raw materials. The MnFe2O4-CQDs composite materials were then used as electrode modification materials to prepare a MIPES for the detection of CLB. The MIPES was prepared by electropolymerizing 3-aminobenzoic acid as the functional monomer and CLB as the template molecule on the MnFe2O4-CQDs modified electrode surface. The optimized MIPES demonstrated good selectivity and sensitivity for the detection of CLB.

The chemicals, including tetrahydrated manganese chloride, ethylene glycol, chitosan, potassium dihydrogen phosphate, 3-aminophenylboronic acid, hexahydrated ferric chloride, sodium acetate, potassium dihydrogen phosphate, and potassium ferricyanide were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Co., Ltd. CLB was purchased from Shanghai Yeyuan Biochemical Reagent Co., Ltd.

Citrus peel was selected as the carbon source for synthesizing CQDs due to its high carbon content and biocompatibility. Additionally, citrus peel is an abundant and renewable resource, making it a cost-effective and environmentally friendly option for CQD synthesis. The functional groups on the surface of CQDs derived from citrus peel, such as carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, contribute to their solubility in water and compatibility with various composite materials, which facilitates their application in electrochemical sensing.

To synthesize CQDs from citrus peel, the dried and ground citrus peel was first refluxed in an aqueous solution of nitric acid and sulfuric acid (3:1 v/v) at 80 °C for 12 h. The resulting mixture was neutralized with sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and then subjected to centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 15 min to remove the solid residue. The supernatant was collected and dialyzed against deionized water using a dialysis membrane with a molecular weight cut-off of 1000 Da for 48 h. Finally, the CQDs were obtained by lyophilizing the dialyzed solution.

To synthesize MnFe2O4, manganese(II) chloride tetrahydrate (MnCl2·4H2O) and iron(III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O) were used as precursors. A stoichiometric mixture of the precursors was dissolved in deionized water and then mixed with an aqueous solution of sodium hydroxide (NaOH) under constant stirring to form a precipitate. The resulting mixture was heated at 80 °C for 2 h, followed by washing with deionized water and ethanol several times. The washed precipitate was dried at 60 °C for 12 h and calcined at 600 °C for 2 h to obtain MnFe2O4 nanoparticles.

For the preparation of MnFe2O4-CQDs composite materials, a one-step solvothermal method was employed. MnFe2O4 nanoparticles and CQDs were mixed in a solvent mixture of deionized water and ethanol (1:1 v/v) under constant stirring. The mixture was then transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the product was collected, washed with deionized water and ethanol, and dried at 60 °C for 12 h to obtain MnFe2O4-CQDs composite materials.

The MnFe2O4-CQDs composite material was dispersed in deionized water, and the dispersion was mixed with 1 % chitosan at a volume ratio of 3:1. The mixture was then drop-coated onto a polished electrode and dried in an oven for 15 min to obtain the MnFe2O4-CQDs-CS/GCE modified electrode. The modified electrode was placed in a PBS solution containing 1 mM CLB and 1 mM 3-aminophenylboronic acid (pH 9) for electrochemical polymerization of molecularly imprinted membranes (MIPs) using CV. The voltage range was set from 0 V to 1.2 V, the scanning cycles were 10, and the scanning rate was 50 mV/s. The modified electrode was air-dried and then washed with a 0.2 M sodium acetate-acetic acid solution to obtain the MIP-based electrochemical sensor (MIPE/MnFe2O4-CQDs-CS/GCE). A non-imprinted electrochemical sensor (NIP/MnFe2O4-CQDs-CS/GCE) was prepared using the same method without the template molecule.

The MnFe2O4, and MnFe2O4-CQDs composite materials were characterized by FTIR, as shown in Fig. 1. The absorption peaks observed at 3421 cm−1, 3404 cm−1, 1601 cm−1, and 1569 cm−1 for CQDs, MnFe2O4, and MnFe2O4-CQDs composite materials may be attributed to surface -OH and adsorbed water molecules [38]. Additionally, the broad peak at 3000–3500 cm−1 may also include the stretching vibration of N-H [39,40]. The stretching vibration of -CH appeared at 2953 cm−1 for CODs and at 2945 cm−1 for MnFe2O4-CQDs composite materials. The characteristic peaks at around 878 cm−1 and 1036 cm−1 may be attributed to the bending vibration of -CH2 and the stretching vibration of C-O, respectively. The peak at 1407 cm−1 for CQDs and MnFe2O4-CQDs composite materials may be due to the symmetric stretching vibration of O-C-O or the stretching vibration of C-N [41]. Unlike CQDs, MnFe2O4 and MnFe2O4-CQDs composite materials exhibited the intrinsic vibrational peaks of the iron oxide at 572 cm−1 and 621 cm−1, respectively, which are associated with M-O vibrations [42]. These results indicate the successful synthesis of MnFe2O4-CQDs composite materials.

The prepared MnFe2O4 and MnFe2O4-CQDs composite materials were analyzed by XRD. As shown in Fig. 2, the diffraction peaks at 20–30.1°, 35.5°, 56.9°, 62.5°, 73.8° corresponded to MnFe2O4 (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), (440), and (533) crystal planes (JCPDS N. 73.1964). It is worth noting that the (002) diffraction peak of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) at 2θ = 22.3° appeared in the MnFe2O4 composite material [43].

In this study, the electrode modification process was characterized using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). A series of electrodes were prepared, including Bare GCE, MnFe2O4-CQDs-CS/GCE, MnFe2O4-CS/GCE, CQDs-CS/GCE, MIP/MnFe2O4-CQDs-CS/GCE, NIP/MnFe2O4-CQDs-CS/GCE, and MIP/MnFe2O4-CQDs-CS/GCE with 50 μM CLB adsorption. As shown in Fig. 3, compared with Bare GCE, MnFe2O4-CQDs-CS/GCE exhibited a small semicircular arc, indicating a low charge transfer resistance in the electrolyte solution, which demonstrated the good conductivity of MnFe2O4-CQDs composite material and provided material support for the construction of molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensors [44]. The impedance value of CLB-MIP/MnFe2O4-CQDs-CS/GCE reached its maximum when the MIP was coated on the MnFe2O4-CQDs-CS/GCE, indicating that electronic transfer was hindered. However, after the CLB molecules were extracted, the impedance value of MIP/MnFe2O4-CQDs-CS/GCE decreased, indicating that holes appeared in the molecularly imprinted membrane [45], and electrochemical signals could be detected. When the electrode of MIP/MnFe2O4-CQDs-CS/GCE was immersed in a 50 μM CLB solution for adsorption, the impedance value increased.

When preparing a molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensor, selecting the optimal ratio of template molecule to functional monomer is particularly important. The modified electrodes were placed in solutions containing different molar ratios of CLB:3-aminophenylboronic acid (1:2, 1:1, 2:1) for electropolymerization. The current values of MIP/MnFe2O4-CQDs-CS/GCE and NIP/MnFe2O4-CQDs-CS/GCE were measured by CV in probe solution (pH 7). As shown in Fig. 4A, the largest difference in current was observed when the molar ratio of CLB to 3-aminophenylboronic acid was 1:1. Therefore, for the subsequent performance studies of the molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensor, a template molecule to functional monomer ratio of 1:1 was selected.

When preparing MIP/MnFe2O4-CQDs-CS/GCE, it is important to select an appropriate elution time. As shown in Fig. 4B, the current difference increases with increasing elution time, and a stable oxidation-reduction peak is obtained when the elution cycle is 50, with the maximum current difference. The optimal elution cycle of 50 was chosen because fewer cycles may not effectively remove all of the template molecules, resulting in incomplete formation of the molecularly imprinted sites and reduced sensitivity. On the other hand, an excessive number of cycles could cause over-elution, which might damage the imprinted sites or the structure of the composite material, ultimately leading to decreased sensor performance [46]. Therefore, the optimal elution cycle was chosen as 50 cycles to achieve a balance between efficient template removal and maintaining the integrity of the imprinted sites and composite material structure.

The appropriate selection of adsorption time is critical in the preparation of the MIP/MnFe2O4-CQDs-CS/GCE. The sensor was washed and then placed in a 50 μM CLB solution to examine the adsorption time. As shown in Fig. 4C, the overall trend of the current difference was relatively stable between 8 and 30 min. The maximum current difference was obtained at 15 min of adsorption time, therefore, this time was selected as the optimal adsorption time. Shorter adsorption times may not allow for sufficient interaction between the target molecules and the imprinted sites, leading to a lower signal response and reduced sensitivity. On the other hand, excessively long adsorption times might result in saturation of the imprinted sites, with no further increase in the signal response, and could also increase the possibility of non-specific adsorption, negatively affecting the selectivity of the sensor [47]. Thus, an optimal adsorption time of 15 min was chosen to achieve the best balance between sensitivity and selectivity.

The optimized sensor was immersed in CLB solutions of varying concentrations for more than 30 min to reach equilibrium before conducting DPV testing. As shown in Fig. 5A, with an increase in CLB concentration, the peak current gradually decreases. This is because at higher concentrations, more imprinting sites are occupied, resulting in fewer probe molecules passing through the imprinting membrane. Fig. 5B shows the logarithmic relationship between the peak current difference and the concentration of CLB solution. Within the concentration range of 10 nM to 10 mM, there is a good linear relationship between the logarithm of the peak current difference and the concentration. The limit of detection (LOD) is 3.4 nM (S/N = 3). To further demonstrate the performance of our MIP/MnFe2O4-CQDs-CS/GCE sensor, we have compared its characteristics with other sensors reported in the literature for CLB detection. The comparison is presented in Table 1. As shown in Table 1, our MIP/MnFe2O4-CQDs-CS/GCE sensor exhibits competitive performance compared to other sensors reported in the literature. The easy preparation, high sensitivity, wide linear range, and specific recognition response make our sensor a promising candidate for CLB detection in meat samples.".

The stability, repeatability, and reproducibility of the prepared MIP/MnFe2O4-CQDs-CS/GCE sensor were tested. It was found that the sensor could be stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C, and after 18 days, the current value was 86.67 % of the initial value. The same MIP/MnFe2O4-CQDs-CS/GCE was used to adsorb 50 μM CLB for 15 min and repeated DPV testing was conducted 15 times, with an RSD of 2.34 %. Three different MIP/MnFe2O4-CQDs-CS/GCE sensors were used to adsorb 50 μM CLB and test DPV, with an RSD of 4.32 %. These experimental results indicate that the MIP/MnFe2O4-CQDs-CS/GCE sensor has good stability, repeatability, and reproducibility.

In real-world applications, it is essential to evaluate the sensor's selectivity and its ability to resist interference from other molecules. To assess the MIP/MnFe2O4-CQDs-CS/GCE sensor's selectivity, we conducted experiments in the presence of common interferents, such as salbutamol, ractopamine, and dopamine. These molecules were chosen because they have similar structures and functional groups as clenbuterol, which might lead to non-specific binding to the MIP. The MIP/MnFe2O4-CQDs-CS/GCE sensor was exposed to a mixed solution containing 50 μM CLB and 50 μM of each interferent. The obtained current responses were compared with those obtained in the presence of 50 μM CLB alone. The results showed that the current response of the sensor was minimally affected by the presence of these interferents, with a relative change of less than 5 %. This finding indicates that the MIP/MnFe2O4-CQDs-CS/GCE sensor is highly selective for CLB and can effectively resist interference from other structurally similar molecules. The molecular imprinting process, which creates specific binding sites for CLB in the polymer matrix, is the main factor contributing to the sensor's excellent selectivity.

The developed MIP/MnFe2O4-CQDs-CS/GCE sensor, with its high sensitivity and selectivity, has potential applications in various fields beyond clenbuterol detection in meat samples. In clinical diagnostics, the sensor could be adapted for detecting biomarkers or specific molecules related to different diseases, allowing for early diagnosis and improved patient outcomes. In food safety, the sensor can be used to monitor the presence of other harmful substances, such as pesticides, veterinary drug residues, or foodborne pathogens. Finally, in environmental monitoring, the sensor can be employed to detect environmental pollutants or contaminants, facilitating better control and regulation of environmental quality. The versatility and adaptability of the MIP/MnFe2O4-CQDs-CS/GCE sensor make it a promising tool for addressing various analytical challenges in these fields.

In this study, a one-step solvothermal reaction was used to synthesize MIP/MnFe2O4-CQDs-CS/GCE, in which citrus peel was used as the carbon source for CQDs. The material was used to modify the electrode and combined with molecular imprinting technology to prepare a molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensor capable of detecting glucose. The study found that under the optimal conditions, the peak current gradually decreased with increasing CLB concentration in the range of 10 nM to 10 mM. The difference in current AI was linearly related to the logarithm of CLB concentration (LogC), and the LOD was calculated to be 0.041 μM. The prepared MIP/MnFe2O4-CQDs-CS/GCE sensor is easy to prepare, has high sensitivity, a wide linear range, and specific recognition response to CLB molecules.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A label-free aptasensor for clenbuterol detection based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer between graphene oxide and rhodamine B
RSC Adv.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Xiao, L. Sun, M. Kang, Z. Dong, A label-free aptasensor for clenbuterol detection based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer between graphene oxide and rhodamine B, RSC Advances. 12 (2022) 32737–32743.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A label-free aptasensor for rapid detection of clenbuterol based on SYBR Green I
New J. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Xiao, L. Sun, J. Lu, Z. Dong, A label-free aptasensor for rapid detection of clenbuterol based on SYBR Green I, New Journal of Chemistry. 46 (2022) 16177–16182.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel electrochemical sensor based on MoS2 electrospun nanofibers and polyoxometalate composite for the simultaneous detection of ractopamine and clenbuterol
Microchem. J.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Wang, M. Wang, N. Zhang, X. Huang, X. Wang, S. Wang, A novel electrochemical sensor based on MoS2 electrospun nanofibers and polyoxometalate composite for the simultaneous detection of ractopamine and clenbuterol, Microchemical Journal. 189 (2023) 108434. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.108434.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An electrochemical sensor for quantitation of the oral health care agent chlorogenic acid based on bimetallic nanowires with functionalized reduced graphene oxide nanohybrids
ACS Omega

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Li, X. Deng, Z. Wu, L. Zhang, J. Jiao, An electrochemical sensor for quantitation of the oral health care agent chlorogenic acid based on bimetallic nanowires with functionalized reduced graphene oxide nanohybrids, ACS Omega. 7 (2022) 4614–4623.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Application of molecularly imprinted electrochemical biomimetic sensors for detecting small molecule food contaminants
Polymers

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Shao, J. Duan, M. Wang, J. Cao, Y. She, Z. Cao, G. Li, F. Jin, J. Wang, A. Abd El-Aty, Application of Molecularly Imprinted Electrochemical Biomimetic Sensors for Detecting Small Molecule Food Contaminants, Polymers. 15 (2022) 187.
